preamble: The shift from the voiceless dental fricative [θ] to the voiceless stop [t] is known as stopping. It is a very common dialectal variation, particularly in languages that lack dental fricatives in their core phonology.

examples.note: In contrast to Hebrew (which shifted *θ to [ʃ]) and Arabic (which kept [θ]), Aramaic shifted the interdental fricative to the dental stop [t].

Standard Modern Hebrew has lost the distinction between the stop and fricative variants of the letter Tāw, realizing both as [t] (partly due to the influence of Yiddish and other languages without dental fricatives).
In the Greek dialects of Southern Italy, the dental fricatives [θ] and [ð] often shift to stops [t] and [d] under the influence of the local Romance dialects.
Dental stopping is a defining feature of Hiberno-English.

sources: Ladefoged, P., & Maddieson, I. (1996). The Sounds of the World's Languages.
Wells, J. C. (1982). Accents of English.
Huehnergard, J. (2019). Proto-Semitic Language and Culture.
Horrocks, G. (2010). Greek: A History of the Language and its Speakers.
